metadata.title: Claude + Linkedin.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The end state is one slash command that generates five voice-matched LinkedIn posts with image direction. Step one: use Apify to scrape your own public posts and export them to a CSV of raw material. Step two: take the CSV to Cowork and run a prompt that returns a decision-ready coaching brief, not a summary. Step three: turn that report into a Skill, a file with a name, trigger, and instructions, so posts improve the loop.

props.artifactTitle: A LinkedIn skill in your voice
artifactLines: The end state is one slash command that generates five voice-matched LinkedIn posts with image direction.
Step one: use Apify to scrape your own public posts and export them to a CSV of raw material.
Step two: take the CSV to Cowork and run a prompt that returns a decision-ready coaching brief, not a summary.
Step three: turn that report into a Skill, a file with a name, trigger, and instructions, so posts improve the loop.

beats.narration_text: The self-reinforcing loop, better posts train Claude, which writes better posts, is what I want to actually set up, not just admire. Walk me through (1) exactly what fields I'd pull from the Apify CSV export that matter for voice, beyond just the post text, (2) how to write the Cowork analysis prompt so it returns a coaching brief on engagement patterns instead of a bland summary, and (3) what the trigger command and instructions inside the resulting Skill should contain so it reliably outputs five voice-matched post options.

props.command: The self-reinforcing loop, better posts train Claude, which writes better posts, is what I want to actually set up, not just admire. Walk me through (1) exactly what fields I'd pull from the Apify CSV export that matter for voice, beyond just the post text, (2) how to write the Cowork analysis prompt so it returns a coaching brief on engagement patterns instead of a bland summary, and (3) what the trigger command and instructions inside the resulting Skill should contain so it reliably outputs five voice-matched post options.

beats.narration_text: The end state first, so you know what you're building. You call a Skill inside Claude — one slash command — and Claude generates five LinkedIn post options calibrated to your voice, complete with image direction and a suggested angle. Not from the internet. From your best posts. The loop reinforces itself: better posts train Claude better, which writes better posts. Here's how you get there in four steps.

artifactLines: Step 1: Extract your LinkedIn posts via Apify — safely, legally, ~$2 for 1,000 posts.
Step 2: Download as a spreadsheet (CSV or XLSX). Save it.
Step 3: Upload to Claude Cowork with an analysis prompt. Claude builds a report.
Step 4: Create a /linkedin Skill from your best posts. Call it anytime.

beats.narration_text: Step one: extract your LinkedIn posts without touching your account. One practical option is Apify, a web-scraping tool for collecting public posts. Your posts are public on the web, so this is legitimate. Create a free account — you get five dollars of credits, which covers two thousand posts. Find the LinkedIn profile posts agent inside Apify, add your profile URL, save and start. A several-hundred-post export can cost roughly a dollar. Wait a few minutes, then export to CSV. That file is your raw material.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B02.png (screenshot of the Apify LinkedIn profile posts agent interface showing the profile URL input and the export/results screen)

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Step one: extract your LinkedIn posts without touching your account. One practical option is Apify, a web-scraping tool for collecting public posts. Your posts

beats.narration_text: Step two: take the spreadsheet to Claude Cowork and run the analysis prompt. the prompt is specific — it's not 'analyze my posts.' It asks for a decision-ready report: what's actually working, engagement patterns, what to double down on. The goal is not a summary. The goal is a coaching brief. Claude reads all the rows, identifies patterns across hundreds of posts, and outputs a structured report you can act on. This is where Cowork earns its cost — a regular Claude chat would lose context after a few hundred rows. Cowork holds the whole spreadsheet.

props.title: cowork-linkedin-analysis-prompt.md
props.code:
I'm going to give you an Apify LinkedIn profile posts export —
a CSV or XLSX of one person's LinkedIn posts (50–5,000+ rows). I coach this person on content and need a decision-ready report
on what's actually working, so I can tell them what to double
down on and what to drop. Before building, list your top 10 assumptions so I can
sanity-check them, then execute.

beats.narration_text: Step three: turn that report into a Claude Skill. A Skill is a file — a name, a trigger command, and instructions. the LinkedIn Skill tells Claude: here are my top patterns, my voice rules, my banned phrases, and my best-performing post structures. From this point, typing /linkedin-post gives Claude everything it needs to write in your voice without any new context. The key difference from a regular prompt: a Skill is permanent and portable. You don't re-paste it. You load it once and it's there every time.

props.title: linkedin-post/SKILL.md
props.code:
---
name: linkedin-post
description: > Write LinkedIn post options in my voice. Use when I type /linkedin-post or ask for a LinkedIn draft.
--- # Voice: first-person, one idea per post, hook up top.
# Best patterns: contrarian takes, before/after, numbers.
# Ban: hashtag stacks, 'I'm excited to announce', emojis.
# Output: 5 options, each with angle + image direction.

beats.narration_text: What the Skill produces when you call it. Call /linkedin-post with a brief describing the subject and goal. Claude outputs five post options: different angles, different formats, different hooks. Each one comes with a suggested image and a note on what could make it stronger. Pick the option that fits. The optimization is only from your own posts — not from a generic LinkedIn best-practices dataset from the internet, which is probably outdated. This is the difference between a fine-tuned system and a generic prompt.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B05.png (screenshot of Claude generating 5 LinkedIn post options using the /linkedin-post skill, showing the options with angles and image suggestions)

build.suggested: suggested prompt: What the Skill produces when you call it. Call /linkedin-post with a brief describing the subject and goal. Claude outputs five post options: different angles,

beats.narration_text: The system compounds. When a post performs well — high engagement, good comments — add it to the Skill file. The Skill gets a little better. Better posts get trained. Better outputs follow. Repeat the Apify export periodically to update the dataset. The honest caveat: you need at least thirty posts for this to be useful. Below that, there's not enough pattern for Claude to work with. The method can be demonstrated with Alex Hormozi's posts — a public account with hundreds of posts — to show the system before applying it to your own archive.

props.artifactHeading: How the system gets better over time
artifactLines: Post performs well → add it to the Skill file → Skill gets smarter.
Re-run Apify every few months → updated training data → better patterns.
Minimum viable dataset: 30+ posts. Below that, not enough signal.
The system rewards consistency: more posts → better voice model.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Don't start with Apify yet — start by writing the voice rules from memory. This prompt builds a rough version of your LinkedIn Skill from scratch, using questions instead of a spreadsheet. You can add the scraped data later.

props.command: I want to build a /linkedin-post Claude Skill from my writing patterns. Ask me 10 questions about my voice, my best posts, what I never write, and what always performs. Then write me a complete SKILL.md file I can load immediately.
